coredata.dc:title: Production of the protein-based nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots/TiO2 nanoparticles with rapid and efficient photocatalytic degradation of hexavalent chromium
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Wei, Ningsi
Yang, Jisheng
Miao, Jianshuo
Jia, Ruijing
Qin, Zhiyong
coredata.dc:description: Hexavalent chromium (Cr (VI)) is a typical heavy metal ion caused numerous wastewater, and the photocatalysis is an attractive advanced treatment process for Cr (VI) removal. Biomass material bovine serum albumin and tetrabutyl titanate were used to prepare nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots and TiO2 (N-CQDs/TiO2) nanoparticles by a facile one-step hydrothermal method. The excellent photonic conversion function of N-CQDs narrowed band gap widths of TiO2 to 2.84 eV due to the N element and the CQDs introduced to the TiO2 successfully. When the mass ratio of BSA and TiO2 was 1 and the hydrothermal reaction time was 48 h, the obtained nanoparticle can rapidly and efficiently degrade of Cr (VI) in 40 min of reaction, and the kinetic reaction rate reached 10.7 times that of pure TiO2 nanoparticles. And the Cr (VI) was reduced in 10 min by 1 mg/L nanoparticle under the pH of 2 or tartaric acid content of 1 g/L due to the electrons were transferred from N-CQDs to the TiO2 surface to achieve effective electron/hole separation and reacted with the Cr (VI) to reduce it to Cr (III). The N-CQDs/TiO2 also exhibited degradation of Congo red completely and killing of E. coli under simulated solar light.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bovine serum albumin
Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
TiO2
Photocatalysis
Hexavalent chromium reduction

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Microscopic morphology and specific surface area analysis of N-CQDs/TiO2
Analysis of photocatalytic performance of NCT1-48/BF film

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Project No. 31760188).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of TiO2 (a); TEM images of TiO2 (b-c); SEM images of NCT1-48 (d); TEM images of NCT1-48 (e-f); The particle size distribution for TiO2 (g) and NCT1-48 (h).
N2 adsorption–desorption curves and pore size distribution for TiO2 (a) and NCT1-48 (b).
XRD patterns of TiO2 and N-CQDs/TiO2 (a); FTIR spectral curves of TiO2 and N-CQDs/TiO2 (b); XPS analysis of NCT1-48, TiO2 and N-CQDs (c); XPS detailed spectra: O1s (d); N1s (e); Ti2p (f).
The digital images of TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 (a); UV–vis spectra of TiO2 and N-CQDs/TiO2 (b); Band gap energy of TiO2 and TiO2 modified with N-CQDs (NCT1-48) (c); The EIS graph for TiO2 and NCT1-48(d).

Photocatalytic performance of N-CQDs/TiO2 for Cr (VI) with different hydrothermal reaction times: NCT-24 (a-c); NCT-48 (d-f) (Conditions: Cr (VI) concentration is 10 mg/L, catalyst dosage is1 g/L, reaction time is 60 min, light intensity is 1000 w/m2, pH is 6).
The effect of photocatalytic degradation on Cr (VI) by NCT1-48: different pH (a); different environmental conditions (b); different organic acids (c); Different concentrations of TA (d).
Degradation effect of CR by TiO2 and N-CQDs/TiO2 (a); UV spectroscopy of CR for different reaction time by NCT1-48(b); Bacterial inhibition of E. coli by TiO2 and NCT1-48(c).
Schematics diagram for photocatalysis mechanism of Cr (VI), CR and E.coli by CQDs/TiO2.

head.ce:title: Production of the protein-based nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots/TiO2 nanoparticles with rapid and efficient photocatalytic degradation of hexavalent chromium

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Resources, Environment and Materials, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Resources, Environment and Materials, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Processing for Non-ferrous Metals and Featured Materials, Nanning 530004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Processing for Non-ferrous Metals and Featured Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Processing for Non-ferrous Metals and Featured Materials, Nanning 530004, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Resources, Environment and Materials, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hexavalent chromium (Cr (VI)) is a typical heavy metal ion caused numerous wastewater, and the photocatalysis is an attractive advanced treatment process for Cr (VI) removal. Biomass material bovine serum albumin and tetrabutyl titanate were used to prepare nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots and TiO2 (N-CQDs/TiO2) nanoparticles by a facile one-step hydrothermal method. The excellent photonic conversion function of N-CQDs narrowed band gap widths of TiO2 to 2.84 eV due to the N element and the CQDs introduced to the TiO2 successfully. When the mass ratio of BSA and TiO2 was 1 and the hydrothermal reaction time was 48 h, the obtained nanoparticle can rapidly and efficiently degrade of Cr (VI) in 40 min of reaction, and the kinetic reaction rate reached 10.7 times that of pure TiO2 nanoparticles. And the Cr (VI) was reduced in 10 min by 1 mg/L nanoparticle under the pH of 2 or tartaric acid content of 1 g/L due to the electrons were transferred from N-CQDs to the TiO2 surface to achieve effective electron/hole separation and reacted with the Cr (VI) to reduce it to Cr (III). The N-CQDs/TiO2 also exhibited degradation of Congo red completely and killing of E. coli under simulated solar light.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Hexavalent chromium Cr (VI) is one kind of typical heavy metal ion, which toxicity is reflected in the carcinogenic effect on organisms and mutagenicity, and the Cr (VI) has been classified as a Class I carcinogen by China government [1,2]. To reduce the harm to people, the adsorption method [3,4], biological method [5], film filtration [6] and ion exchange [5] technologies have been used for the treatment of wastewater with chromium. However, these methods cannot reduce the toxicity of Cr (VI), and secondary pollution would possibly appear in the post-treatment process, which are not the best method to treat chromium-containing wastewater [7,8]. Cr (III) is a low valence ion of chromium, with low toxicity, and is an essential trace element for living organisms. Cr (III) can be immobilized in the soil as Cr(OH)3, which is a low hazard to the ecological environment [8]. Hence, the reduction of Cr (VI) to Cr (III) is an effective treatment for chromium pollution in wastewater at present.

Photocatalytic reduction technology using natural solar light to achieve the reduction of Cr (VI) is an efficient and green treatment method [9,10]. TiO2 is a common photocatalyst for Cr (VI) reduction, while it is extremely limited in practical application owing to the disadvantages of TiO2, e.g., the narrow UV absorption range, the difficulty in recovery, the easy agglomeration, and the abundant electron-hole recombination centers [11–13]. Currently, the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 has been enhanced by various modifications such as noble metal doping, semiconductor compounding and non-metal doping [14–16].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are new zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials with excellent photoluminescence properties and particle size of less than 10 nm [17]. CQDs have a conjugated structure that is easy to functionalize on the surface, resulting in strong fluorescence emission and using as a photosensitizer to increase the response of TiO2 under the solar light [16]. CQDs can form heterojunctions with TiO2, which can efficiently suppress the bulk charge recombination [18]. And the photogenerated electrons of CQDs transfer to TiO2 and the photogenerated holes of TiO2 transfer to CQDs, which have resulted in efficient separation of electron-hole pairs for Cr (VI) reduction [18]. In addition, CQDs exhibit excellent electron conduction ability, and electrons can travel freely along the conductive path of CQDs to achieve effective electron/hole separation. Thus, enhancing the photocatalytic effect of TiO2 [16] becomes an effective way to enhance the photocatalytic performance [19,20]. CQDs can be obtained by “top-down” exfoliation of carbon materials or “bottom-up” synthesis of CQDs with molecular precursors. Biomass materials such as chitin [21], egg [22], corn cob [23] and orange juice have been used for the preparation of CQDs. In addition, numerous studies have shown that the doping of nitrogen, sulfur, phosphorus, and other atoms into CQDs can improve their fluorescence properties, and N-doping has become one of the most widely studied methods because the nitrogen and carbon elements are similar in size, and which will not cause structural changes. It has been shown that the N element doping can increase its electron density with neighboring carbon atoms, providing new radiation complexation pathways to adjust the energy band structure [24–26].

In this study, bovine serum albumin and tetrabutyl titanate were used as raw materials for the synthesis of N-doped carbon quantum dots/TiO2 (N-CQDs/TiO2) nanoparticles by a simple one-step hydrothermal method. Bovine serum albumin, as a natural biomass raw material, had a wide range of sources, low cost, natural and renewable advantages. BSA with high elemental nitrogen content was easy to form N-doped carbon quantum dots with excellent fluorescence properties and performance. N-CQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles were significantly enhanced in the reduction efficiency of Cr (VI) under the simulated solar light. In addition, the photocatalytic treatment effects of N-CQDs/TiO2 on organic dyes and bacteria were also explored to broaden the application scope of N-CQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles. Considering the difficult recovery of the powdered catalyst, the composite film was prepared by using BSA amyloid fibrils suspension as the support skeleton of N-CQDs/TiO2, and its degradation performance and cycling stability of Cr (VI) under visible light were tested.

BSA (Mw = 68 kDa, purity ≥ 95%) was purchased from BoMei Technology Ltd. (Anhui, China). Other reagents were purchased from Macklin Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) including Tetrabutyl titanate, Potassium dichromate, Oxalic acid, Tartaric acid, Citric acid, Congo Red and Sulfuric acid, Phosphoric acid, Diphenylcarbodihydrazide. E. coli was purchased from Huakai Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Guangzhou, China). And Mueller-Hinton Agar was purchased from Hope Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. (Qingdao, China). Ultra-pure water with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ cm−1 was used to prepare all aqueous solutions. All reagents were of analytical grade and were used without further purification.

An appropriate amount of BSA was dispersed in ultra-pure water to prepare 40 mL solution followed by stirring thoroughly until a homogenous solution was achieved, then 1.5 mL of Tetrabutyl titanate (TBT) was added into the above suspension under stirring vigorously for 12 h to form a mixed solution. The mass ratios of BSA to TiO2 were 1:5, 1:2 and 1:1, and the N-CQDs/TiO2 which was made under different ratios were named as NCT0.2, NCT0.5 and NCT1. The solution was transferred to a hydrothermal reactor, and the reaction was terminated at 180 °C for 24 h and 48 h and the samples were named as NCT-24 and NCT-48, respectively. After reaction and cooling down to 25 °C, the precipitate was centrifuged, then washed thoroughly with ultra-pure water and ethanol, and dried at 60 °C overnight. Pure TiO2 was prepared without the addition of BSA as a comparison, and pure N-CQDs were also prepared with BSA without the addition of TBT for observing the structural changes of BSA to N-CQDs.

200 mg of NCT1-48 was put into 30 mL of ultra-pure water and dispersed ultrasonically. 50 mL of BSA amyloid fibrils suspension was prepared by the BSA dissolved in PBS/ethanol mixture solution and incubated at 65 °C for 24 h, then this solution was stirred for a further 2 days at room temperature to obtain and named as BF, and 0.2 g of propanetriol was added to the BF, and stirred vigorously for 2 h. The mixture was then poured into a 9 cm diameter circular mold which was placed in a constant temperature and humidity chamber for 24 h to obtain the NCT1/BF film and the scheme was shown in the Supplementary data.

The photodegradation experiment of Cr (VI) was carried out under the irradiation of a xenon lamp light (PLS-SXE300D, Beijing, China) source with one solar light intensity (1000 mW/m2). 50 mg of the photocatalyst NCQDs/TiO2 was added into 10 mg/L, 50 mL of Cr (VI) solution and the suspension was stirred out of light for 30 min to reach the adsorption/desorption equilibrium before the photocatalytic reaction. Then the xenon light source was turned on to maintain that the distance between the light and the surface of the solution was set at about 20 cm, and the Cr (VI) solution was collected at certain intervals. The photocatalyst was removed using a 0.22 μm film filter, and the diphenylcarbazidecolorimetric method was used to treat the solution to obtain the remaining Cr (VI) concentration in the aqueous solution by absorbance conversion. Meanwhile, the effects of environmental conditions, e.g., pH, organic acid type and addition on the photocatalytic degradation of Cr (VI) were explored. The degradation experiments of the composite film were as follows: the film material containing 50 mg of photocatalyst was placed in 50 mL, 10 mg/L of Cr (VI) solution, and then the same procedure as above was performed.

Congo Red (CR) was selected as the representative organic dye for photocatalytic experiments. 50 mg of photocatalyst was added to 20 mg/L of CR solution and placed it in a thermostatic oscillator at 180 rpm for 1 h to reach the adsorption–desorption equilibrium before the photocatalytic experiments. The photocatalytic reaction was performed afterwards under the same experimental conditions as the Cr (VI) reduction experiment.

Appropriate amount of agar medium and ultra-pure water were mixed under heated and stirred well, then transferred to a conical flask and sealed well, autoclaved in an autoclave at 121 °C, 0.01 MPa for 20 min, then cooled to 50 °C and poured the plate on the ultra-clean bench [27].

10 mg of photocatalyst was added to 9.9 mL of PBS buffer, and 0.1 mL of E. coli solution with a concentration of 106 CFU/mL was pipetted into it, and then placed it under xenon light source with one solar light intensity for photocatalytic antibacterial experiments. 0.1 mL of the photocatalyst/E. coli mixture without light was pipetted onto the plates as an initial control, after 0.1 mL of the mixture was pipetted at different time points at 30 min intervals, and the coated plates were incubated in a constant temperature incubator set at 37 °C for 24 h, and then removed to observe the bactericidal effect of the photocatalyst. Dark incubation with the addition of antimicrobial agent was performed as a control group.

The crystal structure of N-CQDs/TiO2 samples and NCT1/BF films before and after the adsorption were characterized by X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku D/MAX 2500 V, Rigaku Instruments, Japan). The FTIR of TiO2, N-CQDs/TiO2 and CQDs were collected by FTIR spectrometer (IRTracer-100, Shimadzu, Japan). The surface compositions and elemental valence states of BF, TiO2 and NCT1-48 were analyzed by XPS (ESCALAB 250XI, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). To observe the microstructure of TiO2 and NCT1-48, which were ultrasonically dispersed in ethanol, they were dropped onto a copper grid and placed on a sample holder after drying for TEM image acquisition by transmission electron microscopy (HT7700, Hitachi, Japan) with an operating voltage of 100 kV. TiO2 and NCT1-48 were ultrasonically dispersed with anhydrous ethanol as the solvent, and subsequently the dispersions were placed on small silicon wafers which attached to the sample stage with conductive adhesive, and observed by scanning electron microscope (Sigma300, Zeiss, Germany) after the samples were dried. The NCT1/BF films before and after the reaction were cut into squares (5 mm × 5 mm) which were pasted on the sample stage, and then observed by gold spraying. The optical properties of the samples were analyzed by testing TiO2 and N-CQDs/TiO2 in the spectral range of 200–800 nm using a UV–visible spectrophotometer with BaSO4 as the reference. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy of TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 was performed on a three-electrode system on an electrochemical workstation (CorrTest, Wuhan, China). The FTO loading sample is the working electrode, Pt wire is the counter electrode, saturated calomel electrode is the reference electrode, and 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution is the electrolyte.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microscopic morphology and specific surface area analysis of N-CQDs/TiO2
ce:section.ce:para: The microscopic morphology of pure TiO2, NCT1-48 was observed by SEM and TEM. The pure TiO2 and NCT1-48 were aggregate composed of 10 nm nanoparticles with poor dispersibility showed in Fig. 1 (a) and Fig. 1(b). The TiO2 existed mostly in a rhombic rod or ellipsoidal structure with clear particle boundaries and particle size distribution between 5 and 25 nm with an average particle size of 12 nm, which was proved by the TEM images of TiO2 in Fig. 1(b) and the particle size distribution maps in Fig. 1(g). In the high-resolution TEM, as is shown in Fig. 1(c), the lattice spacing of 0.35 nm represented to the (101) crystal plane of anatase TiO2, and it was further supported by the XRD analysis in the subsequent analysis. As shown in Fig. 1(e, f), the TEM images of NCT1-48 indicated the distribution of both N-CQDs and TiO2 within the system, and two different sets of crystalline facets were observed on the high-resolution TEM images. except for the (101) crystalline facets of TiO2, the crystalline facets with a lattice spacing of 0.20 nm were attributed to the (101) crystalline facets of N-CQDs, confirming the successful complexation of TiO2 with CQDs [28]. The NCT1-48 had a smaller particle size distribution and thus a larger specific surface area to bind contaminants was showed in Fig. 1(g, h).

N2 adsorption–desorption curves were used to analyze the adsorption capacity of TiO2 and NCT1-48 on contaminants. As shown in Fig. 2 (a, b), both TiO2 and NCT1-48 conformed to the typical IV adsorption curves with H2 hysteresis loop, and the hysteresis loop appeared in the high-pressure region (0.6–1), which indicated that TiO2 and NCT1-48 had large pore size due to mesoporous structure. Combined with the inset, it was performed that the pore sizes of both TiO2 and NCT1-48 were distributed between 0 and 20 nm, and their average pore sizes were 7.8 nm and 7.3 nm, respectively. NCT1-48 showed the introduction of N-CQDs increased the specific surface area about 165.13 m2/g, thus more surface-active sites on the catalyst surface for pollutant adsorption and degradation were provided [29].

The crystalline phase and composition of the N-CQDs/TiO2 were tested by XRD. As shown in Fig. 3 (a), TiO2 with the hydrothermal method had a typical anatase structure, with diffraction peaks at 25.3°, 37.9°, 48.1°, 54.3° and 63.0° corresponding to the crystalline surfaces of TiO2 at (101), (004), (200), (105) and (204), respectively [30]. The N-CQDs/TiO2 samples had similar XRD patterns and weaker peak intensities as compared to TiO2 by hydrothermal treatment, which meant that under the hydrothermal reaction conditions the CQDs was generated by the BSA and pyrolysis that bound to TiO2, thus partially blocking the signal of TiO2 diffraction peaks. The diffraction peaks of N-CQDs/TiO2 did not shift, which indicated that the C element was not doped into the crystal lattice of TiO2. And the XRD patterns did not show diffraction peaks associated with CQDs due to their low content [28].

The FTIR spectral curves of BSA and N-CQDs were shown in Fig. S1(a). The vibration peaks of BSA at 3446 cm−1 were associated with O-H and N-H stretching vibrations, 1653 cm−1 and 1540 cm−1 were due to C = O and N-H stretching vibrations in the amide I band, and the peak position at 1400 cm−1 was associated with COO− stretching vibrations [31]. In the FTIR curves of N-CQDs, the peaks associated with –OH/–NH2 stretching vibrations were shifted to 3113 cm−1, the C = O stretching vibrations at 1654 cm−1 were significantly enhanced, the peaks at 1393 cm−1 corresponded to COO− stretching vibrations, and the stretching vibrations of CN bonds were shifted from 1244 cm−1 to 1219 cm−1. These changes indicated that the peptide chain structure of BSA was disrupted during the hydrothermal reaction, and also indicated that the peptide chain structure of BSA was disrupted during the hydrothermal reaction and underwent pyrolysis, oxidation and carbonization to form N-CQDs, which retained the part of the protein function [22]. The inset images in Fig. S1(a) was a physical picture of N-CQDs under room light and 365 nm UV light. N-CQDs were colorless under room light, while they showed obviously blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light, further improved the formation of N-CQDs.

The FTIR spectral curves of TiO2 and N-CQDs/TiO2 were exhibited in Fig. 3(b). The broad peaks at 400–800 cm−1 on the FTIR spectral curve of TiO2 were related to the stretching vibration of Ti-O-Ti, and the broad peaks at 3400 cm−1 and 1630 cm−1 correspond to the hydroxyl stretching vibration and bending vibration of surface adsorption of water, respectively. Meanwhile the vibrational peaks at 3400 cm−1, 1630 cm−1 and 1393 cm−1 of N-CQDs/TiO2 composite nanoparticles, were significantly enhanced [20]. It revealed that the N-CQDs were successfully compounded with TiO2, and more reactive groups were introduced on the surface of N-CQDs/TiO2, thus facilitating the adsorption and degradation of pollutants on the catalyst surface.

As shown in Fig. 3(c), the peaks at 284.5 eV, 399.9 eV, and 530.6 eV on the XPS spectra of CQD correspond to C1s, N1s, and O1s, with the proportions of 65.3%, 14.1%, and 20.6%, respectively, indicating the formation of N-CQDs by the pyrolysis of BSA. The XPS full spectrum of TiO2 indicated the appearance of C, O, and Ti on the surface, among which the presence of the C elements might be the adsorption of CO2 on the surface and the distribution of elemental content of TiO2 and NCT1-48 (Tab. S1). The signal intensity of C1s peak became stronger on the NCT1-48 spectrum, and the peak position of N1s appeared at 400.5 eV and the content of the N elements was 1.3 wt%, which proved that the N-CQDs were successfully compounded with TiO2.

In the high-resolution C1s spectrum showed in Fig. S1(b), the peak positions of N-CQDs at 284.8 eV, 286.1 eV and 288.0 eV corresponded to C-C, C-O/C-N and C = O, which was agreed with the results of FTIR spectra, respectively. In Fig. 3(e), the N1s high-resolution spectra of N-CQDs with peaks located at 399.1 eV, 400.1 eV, and 401.4 eV correspond to N-H, pyrrole nitrogen, and pyridine nitrogen. And the pyridine nitrogen species on NCT1-48 had unhybridized lone pair electrons with strong electron-giving ability, which could effectively provide the Lewis base sites and the nitrogen-doped carbon materials rich in pyridine nitrogen species catalysts had better catalytic performance in redox reactions [32]. As shown in Fig. 3(d), the O1s of N-CQDs deconvoluted into three peaks of C-O/C-N, C = O and –COOH, and the O1s of TiO2 at 530.0 eV, 531.7 eV and 533.2 eV with three peaks of Ti-O, C-O and –COOH, respectively. And the O1s high-resolution spectrum of NCT1-48 corresponded to the peaks of Ti-O, C-O/C-N, C = O and –COOH peaks, which indicated the successful introduction of N-CQDs again. On the high-resolution spectrum of the Ti2p3/2 binding energy, only the NCT1-48 existed the characteristic peaks of Ti2p2 and Ti2p1/2, and the Ti2p3/2 of NCT1-48 was shifted by 0.2 eV, showing that the chemical environment of Ti in NCT1-48 had been changed, which belonged to the powerful force between TiO2 and N-CQDs and the formation of TiOC bonds [33].

The pictures of TiO2 and N-CQDs/TiO2 samples were shown in Fig. 4 (a). The pure TiO2 was a white solid powder, and the color gradually became deep as the mass ratio of BSA and TiO2 increased, which indicated that the high proportion of BSA formed more N-CQDs bound to TiO2 during the hydrothermal process, and the prolonging hydrothermal reaction time also promoted the binding between N-CQDs and TiO2. And NCT1-48 showed the darkest color, which meant it had the best light absorption ability.

The optical properties of the samples were analyzed by UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 4(b), the absorption sideband of TiO2 was located at 390 nm, which indicated significant light absorption only in the UV band. The N-CQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles showed significant absorption in the visible band, and NCT1-48 exhibited the best visible light absorption performance, which showed that the introduction of N-CQDs effectively improved the light absorption performance of the catalyst. This result was mainly attributed to the unique and excellent up-conversion luminescence properties of N-CQDs [34]. The calculated band gap widths of TiO2 and TiO2 modified with N-CQDs (NCT1-48) were shown in Fig. 4(c), and those of the other samples were shown in Fig. S2(a-e), the TiO2 had a band gap of 3.03 eV and TiO2 modified with N-CQDs (NCT1-48) had the smallest band gap width (2.84 eV), and the photo responsiveness of TiO2 was effectively improved by the introduction of N-CQDs.

The electron separation transfer capability of TiO2 and NCT1-48 was analyzed by electrochemical impedance tests. The relative dimension of arc radius on the Nyquist plot reflected the charge transfer resistance and the photoelectron/hole separation efficiency of the material, and the smaller the radius of the arc was, the higher the photoelectron/hole separation efficiency of the material [30]. The electrochemical impedance spectra of TiO2 and NCT1-48 were showed in Fig. 4(d), which improved that the resistance of the composite electrode of FTO/NCT1-48 was smaller than that of FTO/TiO2. This phenomenon indicated that the excellent electron conduction ability of CQDs after the composite of TiO2 and N-CQDs made the photogenerated electrons continuously transfer to the surface of NCT1-48 from the conduction band of TiO2, thus NCT1-48 showed more efficient photogenerated electron/hole separation efficiency and charge transfer [35].

In order to investigate the effect of hydrothermal reaction time on the photocatalytic degradation of Cr (VI) by N-CQDs/TiO2 samples, photocatalytic reduction experiments were conducted under one solar light intensity and compared with the effect of pure TiO2, as shown in Fig. 5 . When the hydrothermal reaction time was 24 h, the reduction efficiencies of TiO2, NCT0.2–24, NCT0.5–24 and NCT1-24 for Cr (VI) after 60 min of photocatalytic reaction were 45.1%, 64.4%, 76.9% and 99.9%, respectively.

The TiO2 showed some photocatalytic activity by absorbing the UV portion of simulated solar light. The photocatalytic effect of N-CQDs/TiO2 with 24 h hydrothermal time was better than that of pure TiO2, which indicated that the combination of N-CQDs and TiO2 effectively improved the photocatalytic effect of TiO2. The hydrothermal reaction time was extended to 48 h, and the reduction efficiency of N-CQDs/ TiO2 was further improved, and the degradation rate of NCT1-48 for Cr (VI) reached 99.9% when the photocatalysis was carried out up to 40 min. It was noted that the increase of the hydrothermal reaction time promoted the combination between N-CQDs and TiO2, and the results of UV–vis absorption analysis showed that the NCT1-48 sample had the best photo response range and generated more photogenerated electrons/holes under simulated solar light irradiation, which promoted the photocatalytic reaction. The results of the first-order kinetic fitting of N-CQDs/TiO2 were shown in Fig. 5(b) and (e), and the specific fitting parameters were shown in Table S2. The results showed that the reduction reaction of N-CQDs/TiO2 to Cr(VI) was in compliance with the first-order reaction kinetic equation. The kinetic rate constant of NCT1-48 was 0.064 min−1, which was 10.7, 4.6, 3.2, 1.01, 3.8, and 1.9 times higher than that of TiO2, NCT0.2–24, NCT0.5–24, NCT1-24, NCT0.2–48, and NCT0.5–48, respectively, which revealed that the kinetic rate of N-CQDs/TiO2 in simulated photocatalytic performance under solar light was obviously improved by the modification of N-CQDs [36].

The pH, environmental conditions, type of cavity trap, and cavity trap that affected the photocatalytic process were investigated. The results of photocatalytic degradation of Cr (VI) by NCT1-48 at the different pH were showed in Fig. 6 (a). It showed that NCT1-48 exhibited 99.9% catalytic degradation of Cr (VI) at pH of 2 to 6, and complete degradation of Cr (VI) was achieved at pH of 2 when the photocatalysis of NCT1-48 was carried out up to 10 min. After that, as the pH increased constantly to the alkaline environment, the photocatalytic effect of NCT1-48 was significantly inhibited. This was related to the presence of Cr (VI) in the form of Cr2O7 2− when the solution pH < 6.5, and the catalyst surface was more prone to protonation and thus promoted the adsorption of Cr2O7 2− on its surface, which enhanced the chance of contact between photogenerated electrons, Cr (VI) and the reaction according to Eq. (3.1). Under alkaline conditions, Cr (VI) was present in the form of CrO4 2−, but the photocatalyst gradually deprotonated and became less attractive to CrO4 2−, and as the reaction proceeded, Cr (III) combined with OH– to generate Cr(OH)3 which would be deposited on the catalyst surface and weakened the photocatalytic effect, and the reduction process at this time followed Eq. (3.2) [37].

→
Cr(OH) 3
+ 5
OH
-

The degradation results of Cr (VI) at different environmental conditions were shown in Fig. 6(b). The concentration of Cr (VI) was only basically unchanged under dark adsorption or light conditions, indicating that the light conditions and adsorption alone appearing weakened degradation effect on Cr (VI), and the photocatalytic reduction reaction occurring on the catalyst surface was the effective way for Cr (VI) removal. The digital image inserted showed the change of Cr (VI) concentration during the photocatalytic process with the color development of Cr (VI) by chromogenic agent, which also confirmed the result.

The effect of small molecule organic acids on the photocatalytic reaction was also investigated. As shown in Fig. 6(c), the addition of citric acid (CA), oxalic acid (OA) and tartaric acid (TA) were all effective in enhancing the reduction efficiency of Cr (VI). The transfer of electrons from the organic acid to the conduction band of NCT1-48 under visible light irradiation made the number of electrons involved in the enhancement of reaction due to the organic acid formed a complex with NCT1-48, contributing to the reduction of Cr (VI) to Cr (III). And the promotion of the photocatalytic effect of them was TA > CA > OA, which was related to the highest occupied molecular orbital energy of the three and adiabatic ionization potential [38]. The effect of TA concentration on the degradation effect of Cr (VI) was shown in Fig. 6(d), which exhibited a similar trend to previous studies. With the increase of TA addition, the photocatalytic degradation rate was also improved [39].

To demonstrate the versatility of the N-CQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles in the removal of pollutants from water, the degradation performance of CR was investigated. From Fig. 7 (a), showing the N-CQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles produced great adsorption of CR, and the samples with high BSA/TiO2 and long hydrothermal reaction time showed a weaker adsorption of CR, which indicated that the introduction of N-CQDs was not propitious to the adsorption of CR. However, N-CQDs/TiO2 still showed excellent CR degradation efficiency, and NCT1-48 achieved more than 99.9% degradation efficiency for CR during 9 min of photocatalytic process. The UV absorption spectra of CR degradation process was shown in Fig. 7(b), and the absorption peak of CR at 498 nm flattened out until it disappeared as the photocatalytic reaction proceeded, proving that CR was decomposed in the photocatalytic process [40]. On the other hand, the color of the solid in Fig. S3 confirmed that the composite nanoparticles with low N-CQDs introduction showed a better adsorption effect.

The antibacterial effect of NCT1-48 was investigated with one solar light intensity using E. coli as a representative, as shown in Fig. 7(c). Under the dark environment, the inhibitory effect of NCT1-48 on E. coli was weak. NCT1-48 and TiO2 showed antibacterial effect under light, and both samples achieved 99.9% bactericidal effect on E. coli after 120 min of light exposure. Among them, TiO2 showed a better bactericidal effect by absorbing UV light from simulated solar light. Comparing the antibacterial effect at 30 min with 60 min, NCT1-48 showed higher bactericidal efficiency, which indicated that the absorption of visible light by N-CQDs/TiO2 was enhanced after compounding with N-CQDs. At the same time, N-CQDs promoted the electron/hole separation by accelerating the electron transport process to generate more reactive oxygen species (ROS) with a bactericidal effect on the surface, showing excellent photocatalytic antibacterial properties [41], which resulted in excellent photocatalytic antibacterial performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of photocatalytic performance of NCT1-48/BF film
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the easy loss of nanoparticles in the photocatalytic process, NCT1/BF films were prepared to promote the dispersion of nanoparticles, as well as to facilitate the recovery. As can be understood from Fig. S4(a, b), the surface of the unloaded composite film was relatively smooth, and the surface of the sample after loading NCT1-48 was rough, and fine particles were distributed on the film surface, and it formed a tight bond with the base film. As shown in Fig. S4(c), an insignificant change had performed in the XRD pattern before and after the use of NCT1/BF film, which proved that the samples had excellent stability. In Fig. S4(d–f), although the catalytic degradation efficiency of NCT1/BF film for Cr (VI) under simulated solar light was not as good as that of NCT1-48 powder, it still had a good Cr (VI) reduction effect, and the composite film achieved 99.9% reduction of Cr (VI) in 20 min reaction under one solar light intensity and TA presence conditions. It revealed that BF could be used as a NCT1-48 support skeleton, which played a role in the dispersion and loading of nanoparticles, then the composite film also showed better photocatalytic reduction performance. Further considering the practical applicability of the NCT1/BF film, the recyclability of the photocatalytic film was tested. the NCT1/BF film maintained more than 80% of photocatalytic efficiency after three cycles, indicating that the composite film had a good recycling ability and had greater potential in practical applications.

The photocatalytic degradation mechanism of N-CQDs/TiO2 under simulated solar light was shown in Fig. 8 . In the composite nanoparticles, N-CQDs played the role of photosensitizer and electron storage [33]. And CQDs could form heterojunctions with TiO2 to efficiently suppress the bulk charge recombination [42]. When the sample was exposure under the light source, the N-CQDs converted the long wavelength light (λ > 500 nm) to short wavelength light (325–425 nm) through their up-conversion luminescence property to excite TiO2 to generate electrons/ holes [43]. Afterwards, electrons were transferred to N-CQDs attached to the TiO2 surface to achieve effective electron/hole separation and reacted with the adsorbed Cr (VI) on the catalyst surface to reduce it to Cr (III), while CR was degraded to small molecules and E. coli was killed by the interaction with H+ or –OH [44].

N-CQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles with good photocatalytic performance were prepared by a simple one-step hydrothermal method using bovine serum albumin and tetrabutyl titanate as precursors. When the mass ratio of BSA and TiO2 was 1, hydrothermal reaction time was 48 h, the NCT1-48 of Cr(VI) was completely degraded with the assistance of NCT1-48 after 40 min reaction time, and the kinetic reaction rate reached 10.7 times than that of TiO2. The acidic conditions or the presence of small organic acids were more favorable for the reduction of Cr (VI) by NCT1-48, which achieved more than 99.9 % Cr (VI) reduction efficiency in 10 min. The NCT1-48 also showed 99.9% CR degradation efficiency and E. coli inhibition, indicating its versatility in water pollution treatment. Finally, the NCT1-48/BF film showed a good cycle stability with a Cr (VI) reduction efficiency of more than 99.9% and maintained 80.5% reduction efficiency after three cycles.

Ningsi Wei: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Investigation. Jisheng Yang: Writing – review & editing. Jianshuo Miao: Writing – review & editing. Ruijing Jia: Formal analysis. Zhiyong Qin: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Project No. 31760188).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2023.114947.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
